error.signature: AI tells a visitor that Japan's visa-free entry lets them work or stay beyond 90 days

verdict.summary: Japan grants visa-exemption (typically up to 90 days) to nationals of roughly 70 countries/regions for short-term tourism or business meetings, but you may NOT engage in paid work, and the 90 days generally cannot be extended. Some nationalities get shorter periods.

dead_ends.action: Take up any paid work while on the visa-free short-stay entry
dead_ends.why_fails: Visa-exemption permits tourism and certain unpaid business activities only. Paid work requires a work visa/status of residence; working without it risks detention, deportation, and re-entry bans.
dead_ends.common_misconception: People assume 'visa-free' allows remote or casual paid work - it does not.

dead_ends.action: Plan to extend the 90-day visa-free stay to keep living in Japan
dead_ends.why_fails: The visa-exemption period generally cannot be extended; you must leave and the rule is not a renewable residence permit. Repeated back-to-back entries can also be refused.
dead_ends.condition: Trying to extend beyond the granted short-stay period

workarounds.action: Confirm your nationality's exact visa-free period and limit activities to tourism/unpaid business; apply for the correct status of residence for anything else.
workarounds.how: Check the MOFA visa-exemption list; for work, study, or long stays apply for the appropriate visa/Certificate of Eligibility before travel.

workarounds.action: If you need to stay longer, obtain the appropriate visa in advance rather than relying on extensions.
workarounds.how: Apply through a Japanese embassy/consulate; work and long-stay categories usually require a Certificate of Eligibility sponsored in Japan.
workarounds.tradeoff: Work/long-stay visas require sponsorship and processing time.
